profile.name: Karl W.
profile.quote: Something feels off, but I keep telling myself it’s probably nothing serious.
profile.bio_1: Karl began experiencing unexplained neurological symptoms that disrupted his daily life about ten years agfo. After a years-long diagnostic odyssey involving multiple specialists, he was finally diagnosed with a rare condition.
profile.bio_2: Karl prefers to approach healthcare choices analytically and often researches medical literature and engages heavily in online patient groups before making decisions about his treatment options.
profile.goal1: Understand the full range of treatment options and how they might affect long-term outcomes.
profile.goal2: Maintain his professional productivity and independence despite symptoms.
profile.goal3: Identify clinical trials that could provide earlier access to promising therapies.
profile.barrier1: Conflicting information from different physicians about diagnosis and disease progression.
profile.barrier2: Difficulty balancing medical appointments with a demanding job schedule.
profile.barrier3: Uncertainty about how symptoms may progress and impact his future career.

journey.narrative_description: Karl first notices subtle balance changes and fatigue. He is concerned but assumes it may be age-related. He trusts her providers and believes the issue will be manageable.
journey.quote: Something feels off, but I keep telling myself it’s probably nothing serious.
sentiment_reasons: Symptoms are mild and attributed to normal aging
Karl retains full trust in his care team at this stage
Daily function remains largely intact

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Underlying worry is present but well-managed beneath a surface of optimism.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Karl enters the healthcare system with strong baseline faith in his physicians.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: He can articulate his concerns but lacks a framework for what the symptoms might mean.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Work schedule and geographic access to care are not yet a limiting factor.

journey.narrative_description: When symptoms fluctuate or temporarily improve, Karl feels reassured. Hope increases and He interprets the instability as something benign or reversible.
journey.quote: When it gets a little better, I convince myself I might be overreacting.
sentiment_reasons: Temporary symptom relief creates false reassurance
Fluctuation interpreted as proof the problem is benign
Emotional burden briefly lifts during improvement windows

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Relief from fluctuating symptoms generates genuine, if premature, optimism.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Improvement is attributed to providers' guidance, temporarily reinforcing trust.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Karl feels less urgency to seek information during calm periods, slightly reducing engagement.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Fewer symptoms mean fewer appointments needed, reducing scheduling pressure.

journey.narrative_description: During early appointments, Karl struggles to clearly describe her symptoms. He leaves feeling partially heard but uncertain. Trust begins to soften as explanations feel incomplete.
journey.quote: I’m trying to explain what’s happening, but I’m not sure I’m being understood.
sentiment_reasons: Appointments feel inconclusive and leave questions unanswered
Karl struggles to articulate neurological symptoms accurately
Provider communication feels rushed or incomplete

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Frustration with incomplete answers begins to erode the early optimism.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Karl leaves appointments feeling partially heard but not fully understood.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Difficulty describing symptoms makes him doubt his own ability to communicate effectively.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Appointments are manageable but starting to compete with work obligations.

journey.narrative_description: As symptoms continue progressing, Karl feels discouraged and confused. The lack of clarity lowers her confidence in her own understanding and in the care pathway.

inflection_detail.label: Care Pathway Fork
inflection_detail.description: Worsening symptoms with no clear explanation force a decision: continue with the current provider and wait, or escalate toward specialist care. The choice made here directly determines how long Karl remains undiagnosed.
inflection_detail.sponsor_opportunity: Provide primary care physicians with a structured symptom-progression checklist that triggers automatic neurology referral when motor symptoms persist beyond 90 days.
inflection_detail.data_needed: Median time between first reported motor symptom and specialist referral in community care settings.

paths.label: Escalates to Specialist
paths.outcome: Referral to a movement disorder neurologist is initiated. Diagnostic timeline shortens by an estimated 6–12 months, preserving provider trust and patient confidence.
paths.key_driver: Family pressure and persistent symptom logging prompt action.

paths.label: Waits and Watches
paths.outcome: Continued regression without specialist input deepens despair and erodes medical self-efficacy. Misdiagnosis risk increases significantly.
paths.key_driver: Provider reassurance and logistical friction delay escalation.

journey.quote: Things are getting worse and I still don’t have answers—it’s starting to wear on me.
sentiment_reasons: Continuous worsening without explanation generates despair
Absence of a clear diagnosis creates a sense of system failure
Karl begins questioning his own perception of symptoms

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Progressive decline without answers shifts mood from frustration toward fear and hopelessness.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Repeated inconclusive visits erode confidence in the current care pathway.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Karl loses confidence in his ability to self-advocate or meaningfully influence the trajectory.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Increasing symptom burden begins interfering with work and daily scheduling.

journey.narrative_description: The referral to a neurologist restores cautious optimism. Karl believes expert input may finally provide answers, even though the logistics feel burdensome.
journey.quote: Seeing a specialist finally feels like a step in the right direction.
sentiment_reasons: Specialist referral feels like a meaningful step forward
Karl interprets the referral as validation that his concerns are serious
Anticipation of expert input restores cautious hope

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: The prospect of expert evaluation provides emotional relief after a period of stagnation.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Primary care physician's willingness to refer partially restores confidence in the system.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Karl feels neither more nor less capable — awaiting the specialist's interpretation.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Specialist appointment adds scheduling complexity and potential travel burden.

journey.narrative_description: Receiving an initial but incorrect diagnosis is destabilizing. Karl feels surprised and unsettled. When treatment does not help, doubt grows—both in the system and in her own interpretation of symptoms.
journey.quote: If this diagnosis is wrong, what else have we missed?
sentiment_reasons: Incorrect diagnosis triggers surprise and disorientation
Treatment non-response deepens doubt in clinical judgment
Karl begins questioning whether the system can be trusted to find the answer

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Receiving a wrong diagnosis and watching treatment fail is deeply destabilizing emotionally.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: A misdiagnosis is a concrete breach — Karl's trust in specialist accuracy drops sharply.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Confusion about what is actually happening undermines his ability to participate in decisions.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Treating a wrong diagnosis wastes time, financial resources, and scheduling bandwidth.

journey.narrative_description: Seeking a second opinion feels proactive. Karl regains some agency and begins researching more deeply, determined to reach diagnostic clarity.
journey.quote: I need to take control and make sure we’re not overlooking anything.
sentiment_reasons: Pursuing a second opinion restores a sense of personal agency
Karl feels he is taking control of a process that felt out of his hands
Research-oriented approach aligns with his analytical identity

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Taking proactive steps lifts mood from passive despair toward purposeful engagement.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Trust is rebuilt cautiously — directed at the new provider rather than the system broadly.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Active research and self-advocacy significantly increase Karl's sense of capability.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Second opinion requires additional appointments and potential travel, adding logistical strain.

journey.narrative_description: The Parkinson’s diagnosis brings both relief and grief. Relief that there is finally a label; grief over what it implies. He feels overwhelmed by the long arc of disease progression.

inflection_detail.label: Diagnosis Decision Point
inflection_detail.description: Receiving a confirmed Parkinson's diagnosis is the central inflection of Karl's journey. How He and her care team respond in the immediate aftermath — emotionally, informationally, and logistically — determines whether He moves toward empowered self-management or retreats into isolation and fear.
inflection_detail.sponsor_opportunity: Develop a diagnosis-day support package for HCPs: standardized emotional framing language, a patient-facing 'What happens next' one-pager, and a warm handoff to a patient navigator or advocacy organization.
inflection_detail.data_needed: Rate of clinical trial awareness at time of confirmed PD diagnosis; proportion of patients who receive disease management education within 30 days of diagnosis.

paths.label: Supported Transition to Self-Management
paths.outcome: Karl receives structured post-diagnosis education, is connected to an advocacy community, and begins shared decision-making with her neurologist. Trial awareness is introduced within 60 days.
paths.key_driver: Proactive HCP communication, patient navigator support, and strong family involvement.

paths.label: Emotional Withdrawal and Disengagement
paths.outcome: Without structured support, grief dominates. Karl delays follow-up appointments, withdraws from her caregiver network, and is unlikely to engage with trial options for 12+ months.
paths.key_driver: Information overload at point of diagnosis, lack of emotional scaffolding, and unaddressed logistical concerns.

journey.quote: At least I have a name for it now, but I’m not sure what comes next.
sentiment_reasons: Confirmed diagnosis delivers relief after years of uncertainty
Grief over the chronic, progressive nature of Parkinson's follows quickly
Overwhelming information at point of diagnosis creates cognitive overload

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: The emotional weight of a Parkinson's diagnosis — relief mixed with grief — produces a net negative valence.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Trust in the diagnosing neurologist wavers as Karl processes whether care has been timely enough.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: The scope and complexity of PD management feels far beyond his current knowledge base.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Anticipating long-term specialist visits, medication management, and potential symptom interventions overwhelms near-term planning.

journey.narrative_description: Online communities provide emotional validation and practical insights. Karl feels less alone and gains confidence in navigating future decisions.
journey.quote: Hearing from others like me makes this feel a little less isolating.
sentiment_reasons: Peer connection with others living with Parkinson's reduces isolation
Practical strategies from community members increase confidence
Shared experiences normalize the diagnosis and provide emotional validation

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Community belonging and validation produce a meaningful uplift in overall emotional state.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Positive stories about care experiences in the community reinforce trust in specialist-led treatment.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Absorbing peer knowledge about managing PD symptoms substantially grows Karl's self-management confidence.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Community members share practical workarounds for appointment logistics and medication management.

journey.narrative_description: When evaluating clinical trials, Karl feels cautiously hopeful. He trusts her neurologist but worries about the physical and logistical demands participation may require.
journey.quote: I want to try something new, but I need to be sure I can handle what it takes.
sentiment_reasons: Clinical trial option represents hope for disease-modifying benefit
Karl trusts his neurologist's guidance but is cautious about commitment
Logistical demands of trial participation create real uncertainty

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Cautious hope about potential trial benefit is moderated by anxiety about what participation requires.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Karl relies on his neurologist's recommendation as the primary filter for trial evaluation.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Research experience from earlier stages means Karl can meaningfully evaluate trial options.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Trial visit schedules, geographic constraints, and work balance create tangible participation barriers.

profile.name: Tomáš K.
profile.preference: access Vorasidenib or enroll in IDH1 clinical trial
profile.distance to care: 4–5 hours to Vienna / Prague (nearest specialist centers)
profile.quote: Vorasidenib is approved. I know it exists. My oncologist knows it exists. But in our country it is simply not available, and no one can tell me when that will change.
profile.bio_1: Tomáš was preparing a bid for a large project when he experienced a focal seizure at his desk. He noticed word-finding difficulties and mild headaches for several months prior, which he attributed to work stress. An MRI at his regional hospital in North Macedonia revealed a non-enhancing left frontal mass. He was referred to a neurosurgical center in Skopje, where a gross total resection was performed.
profile.bio_2: Tomáš is an organized, practically oriented professional who responds to uncertainty by seeking information and building plans. He reads medical literature and has joined several international online patient communities where he has encountered accounts of Vorasidenib (Voranigo) access across the EU. His wife manages household logistics and their daughter's school schedule while working part-time. Financially, the family is middle-class — they own their home and have modest savings, but extended out-of-pocket drug costs or frequent international travel would strain them within months. Tomáš is acutely aware that his treatment window — before potential transformation to higher-grade disease — is not infinite.
profile.goal1: Access Vorasidenib as the standard-of-care IDH inhibitor recommended by EU neuro-oncology guidelines, either through named patient programs, cross-border care, or clinical trial enrollment.
profile.goal2: Avoid or substantially delay radiation and chemotherapy, preserving cognitive function for occupational re-entry and active parenting.
profile.goal3: Establish a durable treatment plan before his daughter is old enough to understand the severity of his illness — on his own terms, not dictated by system delays.
profile.barrier1: Vorasidenib has received EMA approval but has not been reimbursed by the North Macedonian national health authority, and no named patient program is available domestically. Out-of-pocket cost is prohibitive at approximately €8,000–€10,000 per month.
profile.barrier2: The nearest centers with active IDH1 trial sites and Vorasidenib access are in Vienna, Prague, and Munich — requiring cross-border travel, time off work, accommodation costs, and separation from his family.
profile.barrier3: His domestic oncology team recommends watchful waiting but has limited familiarity with the IDH inhibitor data and actively discourages cross-border self-referral, creating friction in the patient-provider relationship.

journey.narrative_description: Gross total resection is performed at the neurosurgical center in Skopje. The surgery is uneventful and Tomáš retains full motor and language function. When pathology returns two weeks later confirming IDH1-mutant Grade 2 diffuse astrocytoma, his oncologist presents the result as 'relatively good news' — a slow-growing tumor with a favorable molecular profile. However, the oncologist provides no information about Vorasidenib or IDH-targeted therapies, defaulting immediately to a watchful waiting recommendation. Tomáš leaves the consultation with a diagnosis but no treatment plan and begins researching independently that evening.
journey.quote: They said 'Grade 2, IDH-mutant, favorable prognosis.' They said we could watch and wait. I went home and read everything I could find. By midnight I knew the drug existed. I knew it was approved in Europe. I knew my hospital didn't have it.

events.label: Gross Total Resection — Full Function Preserved
events.description: Maximal safe resection achieved at Skopje neurosurgical center. Tomáš retains full motor and language function post-operatively. Intraoperative neuromonitoring used. He is discharged after four days.
events.tooltip: GTR with full functional preservation — a genuinely positive surgical outcome that establishes initial trust in the Skopje team.

events.label: Oncologist Recommends Watch-and-Wait Without Mentioning Vorasidenib
events.description: At the post-pathology consultation, the oncologist recommends observation with 3-monthly MRI. No mention is made of Vorasidenib, IDH inhibitor therapy, or clinical trial options. Tomáš discovers the existence of Vorasidenib through his own research the same evening.
events.tooltip: Watch-and-wait recommended without any discussion of IDH inhibitor options — Tomáš self-discovers Vorasidenib the night of his pathology disclosure.

sentiment_reasons: Successful surgery with preserved function is a meaningful early positive — but the oncologist's silence on IDH-targeted therapy immediately unsettles Tomáš
Self-discovery of an approved, targeted drug his team never mentioned creates a deep and lasting sense of information inequality
The gap between 'favorable prognosis' framing and the reality of an untreated IDH-mutant tumor begins to widen in his mind immediately

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: The surgical success briefly lifts the emotional baseline, but the omission of IDH inhibitor options in the same consultation deflates it — leaving a net slightly negative state.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Initial trust in the surgical team is solid; the oncology consultation introduces the first significant trust gap through information omission.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Tomáš's practical intelligence activates immediately — he begins structured research within hours of leaving the clinic.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Post-operative recovery is manageable; logistical demands are not yet a dominant concern at this stage.

journey.narrative_description: Tomáš confirms through his oncologist and a pharmacy inquiry that Vorasidenib is not reimbursed in North Macedonia and carries an estimated out-of-pocket cost of €8,000–€10,000 per month. The drug is EMA-approved. It is available in Germany, Austria, and France. His country has not initiated a reimbursement review timeline. He contacts the local patient association for rare cancers and is told there is no named patient program and no compassionate use pathway currently active for vorasidenib. His wife sits with him at the kitchen table while their daughter sleeps and they calculate how many months their savings could cover.
journey.quote: The drug is approved. In Germany they can get it. In France they can get it. We are three hours from Vienna. But for us it does not exist. This is the part that made me genuinely angry.

events.label: Vorasidenib Not Reimbursed — No Named Patient Pathway
events.description: Pharmacist and oncologist confirm Vorasidenib is unobtainable domestically. EMA approval does not translate to national reimbursement. The patient association confirms no compassionate use or named patient program is active in North Macedonia.
events.tooltip: EMA-approved drug unavailable domestically. No reimbursement review timeline. No named patient program. Out-of-pocket cost approximately €8,000–€10,000/month.

events.label: Out-of-Pocket Cost Calculation — Savings Runway Is Limited
events.description: Tomáš and his wife calculate that their household savings could sustain 3–4 months of out-of-pocket Vorasidenib at best. His medical leave has reduced household income by approximately 40%. The financial ceiling makes self-pay a bridge, not a solution.
events.tooltip: Household savings could sustain 3–4 months of self-pay at most. Medical leave has reduced income by ~40%. Out-of-pocket is not a durable solution.

events.label: Country-Specific Access Status Tool — EMA Approval vs. National Reimbursement Map
events.description: A sponsor-maintained, publicly accessible digital tool showing real-time vorasidenib reimbursement status by EU member state — including named patient program availability, estimated processing timelines, and links to country-specific application forms — would intercept Tomáš at the exact moment he discovers the access gap. Rather than ending at 'not available,' the tool would immediately surface his next available option: named patient application, cross-border self-referral, or active trial sites within travel range.
events.tooltip: real-time EU access status map by country — prevents the 'drug exists but is unavailable' dead end by surfacing named patient and trial pathways immediately.

events.label: Financial Bridge Program — 90-Day Self-Pay Coverage While Named Patient Processes
events.description: A sponsor-funded financial bridge program providing vorasidenib at no cost for 60–90 days while a named patient application or trial screening is processed would eliminate the kitchen-table savings calculation as a rate-limiting step. Tomáš's family savings runway of 3–4 months is the primary structural reason self-pay is a bridge rather than a solution. Removing that ceiling during the access processing window transforms a crisis timeline into a manageable one.
events.tooltip: 60–90 day cost-free drug bridge during named patient or trial screening processing — eliminates the savings depletion crisis that forces premature, distressed decisions.

events.label: No Central Resource Explaining Named Patient Process for North Macedonia
events.description: Tomáš cannot find any authoritative, patient-facing resource explaining how a North Macedonian patient would initiate a named patient or compassionate use application for vorasidenib — not from the EMA, the manufacturer, the domestic health authority, or patient advocacy organizations. The gap between EMA approval and domestic access is visible; the pathway to close it is not. This information vacuum is the single largest driver of the path_neg risk at this step.
events.tooltip: Missing resource: no patient-facing guide to named patient application process for non-reimbursing EU countries — EMA approval visible but access pathway invisible.

sentiment_reasons: Discovering that an approved, standard-of-care drug is simply unavailable in his country produces profound anger and a sense of systemic injustice
Calculating the financial runway at the kitchen table with his wife makes the stakes viscerally concrete
The absence of any pathway — named patient, compassionate use, or reimbursement review — removes all domestic options simultaneously

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: This step contains the sharpest single emotional drop in the journey — the transition from 'there is a drug' to 'you cannot have it' is the defining injustice of Tomáš's experience.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The oncologist's failure to proactively raise the reimbursement gap — or offer any pathway guidance — significantly erodes trust in the domestic system.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Tomáš's practical research capability keeps him from feeling entirely passive, but the structural barriers he is encountering are beyond individual agency.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Financial constraint begins to compress logistical options — cross-border access is theoretically possible but cost-prohibitive without additional support.

journey.narrative_description: Tomáš finds an international IDH-mutant glioma patient community on Facebook and a dedicated Reddit subforum. He reads accounts from patients in Georgia and North Macedonia who have traveled to Germany or Austria for second opinions and treatment, and others describing Vorasidenib access through named patient programs in countries where domestic reimbursement was pending. He encounters a Swedish patient who has been on Vorasidenib since September 2024 with 'spectacular' results. He posts his situation and within 48 hours has detailed, practical responses from four patients in similar geographic access situations. He also learns that several centers in Vienna, Munich, and Prague are active EORTC neuro-oncology trial sites.
journey.quote: I posted at midnight. By morning there were four replies from people in almost exactly my situation. One of them had driven from Serbia to Vienna for their consultation. He told me which doctor to ask for. That information did not come from any hospital.

events.label: Finds IDH-Mutant Glioma Patient Community — International Peers
events.description: Tomáš joins an international Facebook group and Reddit community for IDH-mutant glioma patients. He reads accounts from patients in Serbia, Georgia, and North Macedonia who have navigated cross-border access. He identifies a pattern: patients from non-reimbursing countries traveling to Vienna or Munich for second opinions and trial access.
events.tooltip: International patient community reveals a pattern: non-reimbursing country patients traveling to Vienna and Munich for second opinions and trial access — exactly Tomáš's situation.

events.label: Peer Identifies Specific Vienna Neuro-Oncologist with IDH Trial Affiliation
events.description: A patient from Serbia who traveled to the Medical University of Vienna provides the name of a neuro-oncologist who leads an active IDH-targeted EORTC trial. He provides the direct email for the clinical trials coordinator and confirms the center accepts international self-referrals.
events.tooltip: Peer from Serbia provides named neuro-oncologist at Vienna MedUni — EORTC IDH trial affiliation confirmed. Direct coordinator email provided. Center accepts international self-referrals.

sentiment_reasons: Finding peers in genuinely comparable geographic situations — not theoretical ones — produces the first real sense that a path forward exists
The Swedish patient's 'spectacular' Vorasidenib result is the first concrete evidence that the drug works in a real person's life
A named specialist and direct coordinator contact transforms abstract options into actionable next steps

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Community discovery produces the strongest positive shift in the journey so far — grounded in practical information, not reassurance.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Trust shifts from the domestic care system toward the international patient network and the community-identified specialist center.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Accumulating peer knowledge gives Tomáš the vocabulary and framing to begin a productive conversation with a specialist center.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Knowing the specific person to contact at the Vienna center dramatically reduces the logistical complexity of cross-border access.

journey.narrative_description: Tomáš emails the Medical University of Vienna neuro-oncology department directly, attaches his complete pathology report and MRI imaging, and requests a second opinion consultation. He receives an appointment within three weeks. He travels by train with his wife — his daughter stays with her grandparents. The Vienna neuro-oncologist reviews his case in detail, affirms that his molecular profile makes him an ideal candidate for IDH inhibition, confirms that Vorasidenib is available in Austria through national reimbursement, and — critically — informs him of two active clinical trials: an EORTC combination IDH1 + immunotherapy Phase II, and a randomized Phase III comparing vorasidenib against observation in a post-GTR setting with a European site in Prague. The consultation costs €420 out of pocket. Tomáš describes it as the most productive two hours since his diagnosis.
journey.quote: The doctor in Vienna spent ninety minutes with us. She drew diagrams. She printed the trial summaries. She said 'you are exactly the kind of patient these trials were designed for.' I had not heard that before — that I was the right patient for something.

events.label: Vienna Second Opinion — IDH Inhibition Strongly Affirmed
events.description: Vienna neuro-oncologist reviews the case comprehensively and confirms that Tomáš's IDH1-mutant, MGMT-methylated profile makes him an ideal candidate for IDH inhibitor therapy. She provides a written letter of recommendation for Vorasidenib initiation — useful for any subsequent named patient or trial application.
events.tooltip: Vienna specialist strongly affirms IDH inhibition candidacy — written letter of recommendation provided for named patient / trial application use.

events.label: Two Active Trials Identified — EORTC Combination + Phase III Prague Site
events.description: The Vienna oncologist identifies two immediately relevant trials: a Phase II EORTC IDH1 inhibitor + PD-1 immunotherapy combination trial with a Vienna site, and a Phase III randomized trial comparing vorasidenib to active surveillance post-GTR, with a participating center in Prague.
events.tooltip: Two trials identified: EORTC Phase II IDH1+PD-1 at Vienna (combination), and Phase III vorasidenib vs. observation with a Prague site — both within travel range.

events.label: Travel and Accommodation Cost — Vienna Not Sustainable Long-Term
events.description: The Vienna consultation costs €420, plus train travel and one night of accommodation — approximately €700 total. Manageable for a single visit, but a trial requiring monthly visits would cost €600–€800 per month in travel alone, before any drug costs.
events.tooltip: Single Vienna consultation manageable (~€700 all-in). Monthly trial participation would cost €600–€800 in travel alone — a significant recurring burden on a reduced income.

sentiment_reasons: Being told explicitly that his profile is exactly what active trials were designed for is the first moment Tomáš feels like a candidate rather than a problem
Two concrete trial options — within travel range — represent a genuine bifurcation of possible futures
The travel cost reality reasserts itself as a persistent structural constraint even in the most positive consultation of his journey

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: The Vienna consultation is the single most positive step in the journey — combining expert validation, concrete options, and the first sense of genuine clinical partnership.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Trust in the Vienna specialist is immediate and high — she invests time, provides written documentation, and treats his case with visible engagement.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: The structured trial summaries the specialist provides give Tomáš a framework to evaluate options analytically — matching his decision-making style.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: The consultation is logistically achievable; the sustainability of repeated travel at trial-cadence cost is the emerging constraint.

journey.narrative_description: Tomáš returns from Vienna and presents his second opinion and the trial information to his domestic oncologist. The response is dismissive — the oncologist expresses skepticism about the value of the trials ('too experimental') and reiterates a recommendation for adjuvant radiation and temozolomide. He suggests that Vorasidenib's long-term side effect profile is 'unknown' and that proven chemoradiation is the safer choice. Tomáš has read the INDIGO trial data. He knows the NDA. He is not persuaded — but the conflict with his domestic team introduces practical complications: he needs local coordination for monitoring and any cross-border care requires some domestic infrastructure.
journey.quote: My oncologist at home told me the trials are too experimental. He said radiation is proven. But I have read the INDIGO data. I know the five-year progression curves. He did not mention them. How do you trust someone who is telling you less than you already know?

events.label: Domestic Oncologist Rejects Second Opinion — Pushes Radiation
events.description: The domestic oncologist dismisses the Vienna second opinion and the trial options, characterizing them as 'experimental.' He recommends adjuvant radiation and TMZ chemotherapy and expresses concern that pursuing cross-border options will delay treatment. Tomáš questions the framing directly — the oncologist does not engage with the INDIGO data.
events.tooltip: Domestic oncologist dismisses Vienna second opinion and trial options as 'too experimental' — recommends radiation/TMZ, does not engage with INDIGO trial evidence.

events.label: Local Care Dependency — Cannot Fully Disengage from Domestic System
events.description: Tomáš recognizes that regardless of where he pursues treatment, he needs local support: MRI access, levetiracetam prescriptions, and routine monitoring. His domestic oncologist's resistance creates a practical infrastructure problem for any cross-border or trial pathway.
events.tooltip: Cross-border or trial access requires domestic monitoring support. Oncologist's hostility to self-referral creates friction even for logistical coordination.

sentiment_reasons: A physician who argues against a treatment using less evidence than the patient has already read destroys rather than shapes trust
The radiation recommendation is not wrong per se — but presented without any acknowledgment of IDH inhibitor data, it feels like epistemic negligence
Recognizing his dependency on a system that is not supporting him produces a new and specifically frustrating form of anxiety

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: The domestic physician conflict is the second-lowest emotional step — it combines dismissal, power asymmetry, and practical dependency into a highly aversive experience.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The domestic oncologist's failure to engage with primary trial evidence drops provider trust to its lowest point in the journey.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Paradoxically, the conflict reinforces Tomáš's conviction that his own research-led judgment is more clinically grounded than the advice he is receiving locally.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: The dependency on domestic infrastructure for monitoring and prescriptions constrains his options — he cannot fully exit the domestic system even if he wants to.

journey.narrative_description: Armed with the Vienna specialist's letter of recommendation and the patient community's guidance, Tomáš investigates every non-trial pathway to Vorasidenib. He contacts Servier (the manufacturer) directly through their medical information line to ask about expanded access programs. He is informed that a formal expanded access program is not currently active for vorasidenib in his geography, but that named patient applications can be considered on a country-by-country basis and require a local physician to sponsor the request. His domestic oncologist declines to sponsor. His community peers suggest approaching the Vienna specialist to initiate a cross-border named patient application — a process neither party has experience navigating.
journey.quote: I called the company. I was polite. I asked about expanded access. They said it requires a physician sponsor in my country. My physician in my country said no. The circle was complete.

events.label: Expanded Access Application Requires Domestic Physician Sponsor — Refused
events.description: Named patient application for vorasidenib requires a sponsoring physician in the patient's country of residence. Tomáš's domestic oncologist declines to participate, citing unfamiliarity with the application process and personal reservations about IDH inhibitor therapy.
events.tooltip: Named patient application requires domestic physician sponsor. Domestic oncologist declines — citing unfamiliarity and personal reservations. Circle closes.

events.label: Vienna Specialist Agrees to Explore Cross-Border Named Patient Pathway
events.description: The Vienna neuro-oncologist agrees to investigate whether a cross-border named patient application can be initiated from Austria on Tomáš's behalf. This is non-standard and requires coordination between Austrian regulatory authorities and the Macedonian health ministry. No timeline is available.
events.tooltip: Vienna specialist agrees to explore cross-border named patient application — non-standard, requires Austria-North Macedonia regulatory coordination. Timeline unknown.

events.label: International Specialist Letter as Named Patient Sponsor — Bypass Domestic Refusal
events.description: A formal sponsor policy — and supporting regulatory guidance — establishing that an international specialist letter (e.g., from the Vienna neuro-oncologist) can substitute for a domestic physician sponsor signature on named patient applications in non-EU member states would eliminate the blockage Tomáš hits when his domestic oncologist refuses. The Vienna specialist is willing and qualified; the barrier is procedural, not clinical. A pre-negotiated pathway with MALMED (North Macedonia's medicines authority) would make this substitution actionable.
events.tooltip: pre-negotiated named patient pathway accepting international specialist letters in lieu of domestic physician sponsors — eliminates the single point of failure in Tomáš's access route.
events.partner: Medical University of Vienna / MALMED (North Macedonian Medicines Agency)

events.label: HCP Education Program — IDH Inhibitor Data for Domestic Oncologists in Non-Reimbursing Countries
events.description: A targeted medical education program for oncologists in non-reimbursing EU and Western Balkans countries — covering IDH1 inhibitor mechanism, the INDIGO trial data, current EANO guidelines, and how to support cross-border patient referrals — would address the root cause of Tomáš's domestic physician barrier. His oncologist's refusal to co-sign the named patient application is driven by unfamiliarity with the data, not clinical disagreement. Education converts a blocker into a facilitator.
events.tooltip: HCP education program in non-reimbursing Western Balkans markets — converts unfamiliar domestic oncologists from access blockers into referral facilitators.

events.label: No Patient Navigator or Dedicated Access Contact at Sponsor or Trial Site
events.description: At the point of named patient refusal — the most logistically complex and emotionally draining moment in the access journey — Tomáš has no single human contact he can reach out to for navigation support. The Vienna specialist is helpful but not resourced for cross-border regulatory administration. The trial site coordinator handles enrolled participants, not pre-screening access cases. The domestic oncologist has refused to engage. There is no patient navigator, access coordinator, or sponsor helpline that can hold his case and move it forward.
events.tooltip: Missing resource: no dedicated access navigator or case manager for patients in non-reimbursing countries — the most critical gap at the highest-complexity moment in the journey.

sentiment_reasons: The physician sponsor refusal closes every domestic access pathway simultaneously — it is the most complete institutional blockage in the journey
The Vienna specialist's willingness to explore a non-standard workaround sustains hope but introduces a new layer of bureaucratic uncertainty
Tomáš is now managing three parallel tracks — trial, named patient, and self-pay bridge — simultaneously, with no certainty on any of them

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: The named patient rejection and the slow-moving Vienna workaround produce a flat, grinding neutral state — neither the sharp pain of the worst steps nor the lift of genuine progress.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The Vienna specialist's active advocacy partially compensates for domestic system failures; trust is now strongly bifurcated between local and international care.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Tomáš is operating at the edge of what patient-level agency can achieve — he has done everything available to him and is now dependent on institutional action.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Managing three parallel pathways (trial, named patient, self-pay bridge) while on medical leave with a reduced income is beginning to create emotional and logistical overload.

journey.narrative_description: Tomáš focuses his analysis on the two trials the Vienna specialist identified. The Prague Phase III (vorasidenib vs. observation) is closer — a 6-hour drive versus a 9-hour train journey to Vienna. It offers guaranteed vorasidenib access in the active arm, but carries a 50% placebo probability. The Vienna Phase II (IDH1 inhibitor + PD-1 immunotherapy combination) has no placebo arm but is an unproven combination with unknown additive side effects. He creates a comparison document. His wife reads it and asks one question: 'Which one gets you the drug for sure?' He does not have a clean answer. He also connects with a patient in the Prague trial via the online community who shares their visit schedule — approximately one visit every 6 weeks — and confirms the center reimburses reasonable travel costs for trial participants.
journey.quote: My wife asked which trial guarantees the drug. I had to tell her that one has a 50/50 chance of placebo, and the other is a combination that's never been tested. Neither is the simple answer. But both are better than what I have now, which is nothing.

events.label: Peer in Prague Trial Confirms Visit Cadence and Travel Reimbursement
events.description: A community peer currently enrolled in the Prague Phase III trial confirms visits are required every 6 weeks, the site reimburses documented travel up to €200 per visit, and the site coordinator is responsive and English-speaking. This substantially reduces the logistical uncertainty of the Prague option.
events.tooltip: Prague trial peer confirms: 6-week visit cadence, up to €200 travel reimbursement per visit, English-speaking coordinator. Logistical uncertainty substantially reduced.

events.label: 50% Placebo Probability in Phase III — Wife's Core Concern
events.description: The Prague Phase III is a randomized, placebo-controlled trial. Tomáš has a 50% probability of receiving placebo. His wife's reaction to this is visceral — she asks whether they could know if he receives placebo and whether he could withdraw to access the drug another way. He does not yet have those answers.
events.tooltip: 50% placebo probability in Prague Phase III. Wife's central concern: could they know if he receives placebo? Could he withdraw? Answers not yet available.

events.label: Vienna Phase II — Combination Arm Carries Unknown Additive Risk
events.description: The Vienna Phase II adds PD-1 immunotherapy to IDH inhibition — a mechanistically promising but clinically unvalidated combination in glioma. Tomáš researches checkpoint inhibitor toxicity in brain tumors and finds mixed data. The absence of a placebo arm is appealing, but the additive side effect profile is genuinely unknown.
events.tooltip: Vienna Phase II IDH1+PD-1 combination: no placebo arm (positive), but additive toxicity profile unvalidated in glioma context. Mixed literature on checkpoint inhibitors in brain tumors.

sentiment_reasons: The peer contact in the Prague trial makes that option feel real and navigable for the first time
The placebo randomization is not an abstract statistical concern — for a 40-year-old with a six-year-old daughter, it is a concrete fear of spending months unprotected
Facing two imperfect options simultaneously — when the 'right' answer would be unrestricted vorasidenib access — generates a specific kind of frustrated optimism

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Genuine options produce genuine hope — but the imperfections of both options prevent a clean positive shift. The emotional register is engaged uncertainty rather than dread.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The Prague trial peer and the Vienna specialist's continued involvement sustain trust in the research pathway; the domestic system has effectively ceased to be a trust resource.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Tomáš's comparison document and his ability to articulate the mechanistic distinction between the two trials represent the peak of his self-directed health literacy.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Prague's travel reimbursement program meaningfully improves the financial sustainability of trial participation; the 6-week visit cadence is manageable alongside partial work return.

journey.narrative_description: Tomáš faces three possible paths: Enroll in the Phase III; pursue the Vienna Phase II combination; or continue waiting for the named patient pathway to open, bridging with 2–3 months of self-pay vorasidenib if the family depletes part of their savings.
journey.narrative_description_detail: Three converging pressures bring Tomáš to a decision point. His latest MRI — at 7 months post-resection — shows stable disease but his Vienna specialist notes a small area of T2 signal change that she describes as 'worth watching.' His domestic oncologist reiterates that radiation should begin within the next 3 months if they are not proceeding with IDH inhibition. And his daughter, who has been told only that her father has 'something wrong with his head that the doctors are fixing,' asks him one morning whether he will be better by her birthday. He has three paths in front of him: enroll in the Prague Phase III (guaranteed drug access — 50% of the time); pursue the Vienna Phase II combination (no placebo, but unknown additive risk); or continue waiting for the named patient pathway to open, bridging with 2–3 months of self-pay vorasidenib if the family depletes part of their savings. He and his wife schedule an evening to decide.

inflection_detail.label: Three-Way Decision Inflection — Enroll, Wait, or Self-Pay Bridge
inflection_detail.description: Tomáš reaches a convergence of clinical urgency, financial constraint, and family emotional weight that forces a decision without a clear 'right' answer. Each option carries a distinct combination of clinical, financial, and logistical risk. The absence of straightforward domestic vorasidenib access is the structural failure driving this decision — the complexity of his choice is not inherent to his disease state but is an artifact of reimbursement geography. This is precisely the inflection point where sponsor-supported patient navigation and access programs have the highest potential impact on enrollment quality and patient wellbeing.
inflection_detail.sponsor_opportunity: Establish a cross-border patient navigation program for IDH1-mutant glioma patients in non-reimbursing EU countries — providing named patient application support, trial site matching, financial bridge assistance for self-pay periods, and a dedicated international access coordinator who can engage both the patient and the domestic physician. A structured 'access bridge' — covering 2–3 months of vorasidenib at no cost while trial screening or named patient processing proceeds — would materially reduce dropout at this inflection point.
inflection_detail.data_needed: Proportion of IDH1-mutant glioma patients in non-reimbursing EU countries who ultimately access vorasidenib within 12 months of eligibility confirmation; rate of trial enrollment versus watchful waiting in patients who cannot access vorasidenib domestically; average named patient processing time by EU member state; financial distress rate among trial-eligible patients who do not enroll.

paths.label: Prague Phase III Enrollment — Structured Access with Accepted Risk
paths.outcome: Tomáš and his wife decide that the 50% probability of active treatment in a well-run EORTC trial — with travel reimbursement, English-speaking coordinators, and a 6-week visit cadence — is the most sustainable path. They accept the placebo risk as the price of access and commit to Prague. If randomized to placebo, they plan to withdraw at the first MRI and pursue the Vienna Phase II or self-pay bridge. The decision is made with eyes open to its imperfection — and the family frames it as the most active thing they can do together.
paths.key_driver: Prague peer's logistical reassurance; Vienna specialist's written recommendation supporting trial as first choice; family agreement that structured access is preferable to indefinite waiting; site's travel reimbursement reducing financial pressure.

paths.label: Paralysis and Delayed Enrollment — Window Narrowing
paths.outcome: Unable to resolve the placebo concern and without a named patient pathway open, Tomáš and his wife defer the decision. They begin self-pay vorasidenib for one month while waiting — depleting savings at a rate that cannot be sustained. Three months later, the financial runway is exhausted, the named patient pathway has not progressed, and the domestic oncologist's radiation recommendation has become more urgent as a new MRI shows borderline T2 change. Tomáš eventually enrolls in Prague under significantly more emotional and financial duress than would have been necessary three months earlier.
paths.key_driver: Unresolved placebo anxiety; no sponsor-side financial bridge or access navigator; domestic oncologist pressure toward radiation increasing urgency; savings depletion accelerating decision crisis.

journey.quote: My daughter asked me if I would be better by her birthday. It's in four months. I said yes. And then I sat down and thought about what I actually need to do to make that true — not for her birthday, but for the next ten years of them.

events.label: MRI Shows T2 Signal Change — 'Worth Watching'
events.description: 7-month post-resection MRI shows stable tumor volume but a new, small area of T2 signal change. Vienna specialist describes it as 'worth watching' and notes it slightly increases the urgency of initiating IDH inhibition. The finding does not constitute progression, but eliminates the sense that time pressure is low.
events.tooltip: 7-month MRI: stable volume but new T2 signal change. Vienna specialist notes it 'warrants watching' — not progression, but increases urgency of IDH inhibitor initiation.

events.label: Domestic Oncologist Resets Radiation Recommendation — 3-Month Window
events.description: In response to the T2 change, the domestic oncologist restates that adjuvant radiation should begin within 3 months if no IDH inhibitor access is established. He does not acknowledge the trial options. For Tomáš, this feels less like clinical guidance than a deadline issued without understanding his situation.
events.tooltip: Domestic oncologist issues 3-month radiation deadline in response to T2 signal change — does not engage with trial or IDH inhibitor options. Experienced as pressure without understanding.

events.label: Wife and Tomáš Schedule an Evening to Decide Together
events.description: Tomáš and his wife arrange their daughter's bedtime and set aside the evening to review the three options systematically — Prague trial, Vienna trial, self-pay bridge — with Tomáš's comparison document and the Vienna specialist's written recommendation on the table. This deliberate, shared decision-making process is itself a form of reclaimed agency.
events.tooltip: Couple schedules structured decision session with comparison document and Vienna specialist letter — shared deliberation as reclaimed agency.

events.label: Placebo Risk Counseling Tool — Structured Decision Aid for Randomized Trials
events.description: A structured, patient-facing decision aid — covering what placebo randomization means in practice, what monitoring is provided in the control arm, withdrawal rights, and options if randomized to placebo — would directly address the unresolved concern that is blocking Tomáš's enrollment in Prague. His wife's question ('which one gets you the drug for sure?') is the articulation of an anxiety that no clinical document has yet answered in plain language. A one-page decision aid co-developed with patient advocates would close this gap in under 48 hours.
events.tooltip: plain-language placebo risk decision aid — directly addresses the unresolved family anxiety blocking Prague Phase III enrollment at the inflection decision session.
events.partner: Brain Tumour Europe / Rare Cancers Europe

events.label: Peer Patient Program — Connection to Enrolled Prague Trial Participants Pre-Decision
events.description: A formal peer matching program — connecting trial-eligible patients with currently enrolled participants before the enrollment decision — would formalize and scale the accidental peer contact Tomáš made through the online community. The Prague peer's logistical reassurance (6-week cadence, €200 travel reimbursement, English-speaking coordinator) was the single most actionable data point in his decision process. A structured peer program ensures every eligible patient receives this information proactively, not by chance.
events.tooltip: formal pre-enrollment peer matching program — scales the accidental community peer contact that was Tomáš's most decisive logistical reassurance.

events.label: No Domestic Oncologist Willing to Frame Trial as a Valid Clinical Option
events.description: At the most consequential decision point in his journey, Tomáš has no domestic clinical voice endorsing trial enrollment as a valid — let alone preferred — clinical choice. His domestic oncologist is actively recommending radiation within 3 months and has not engaged with the trial options at all. The family is making a decision that requires weighing placebo risk, cross-border logistics, and financial strain without any local physician validation that they are making the right call. This absence of domestic clinical endorsement is the primary driver of decision paralysis on path_neg.
events.tooltip: Missing resource: no domestic oncologist willing to endorse trial enrollment — family makes high-stakes cross-border trial decision without any local clinical validation.

sentiment_reasons: The T2 signal change dissolves any remaining sense that time is abundant — the decision cannot be deferred indefinitely
The daughter's birthday question is not a narrative device — it is the emotional truth of what is at stake, stated without artifice
Scheduling a deliberate decision evening with his wife represents the most autonomous act in the journey — turning a crisis into a choice

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: The convergence of T2 change, radiation deadline, and family emotional weight produces a state of active, loaded uncertainty — not despair, but the weight of a decision that carries irreversible consequences.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Trust in the Vienna specialist and the Prague trial infrastructure remains solid; the domestic oncologist's radiation deadline without acknowledgment of alternatives is experienced as clinical abandonment.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Tomáš's comparison document and structured family decision session demonstrate that he has converted distress into deliberate agency — the hallmark of a high self-efficacy patient under duress.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Self-pay bridge option would deplete savings in 3–4 months; Prague trial travel reimbursement provides the most logistically sustainable path; Vienna is feasible but more expensive per visit.

profile.name: Candace D.
profile.quote: There were months I don't remember at all. I had to learn who I was again — and now I'm trying to figure out if there's something that can actually stop this from coming back.
profile.bio_1: Candace was a second-year PhD candidate when she experienced what her family described as a psychiatric break —  involving paranoia and seizure-like episodes which escalated over several weeks.
profile.bio_2: Candace spent nearly four months inpatient, including three weeks in the ICU. Now in partial recovery, Candace manages residual cognitive fatigue, word-finding difficulties, and anxiety that she and her care team attribute to post-encephalitis syndrome. She has resumed light coursework but is not yet back to full academic capacity. She is highly motivated to understand her disease and advocates for herself in clinical settings, though she sometimes struggles with memory and processing speed during complex conversations.
profile.goal1: Achieve full cognitive recovery and return to her doctoral program without permanent academic setback.
profile.goal2: Reduce relapse risk and understand whether maintenance immunotherapy or a clinical trial offers better long-term protection.
profile.goal3: Rebuild a sense of identity and continuity after a period of significant memory loss and personality disruption.
profile.barrier1: Rarity of the disease means few local neurologists have meaningful experience treating anti-NMDAR encephalitis beyond acute stabilization.
profile.barrier2: Cognitive fatigue and residual word-finding difficulties make it hard to fully engage in complex medical conversations or parse dense clinical trial protocols.
profile.barrier3: Deep anxiety about relapse and uncertainty about whether current stability reflects true remission or temporary suppression.

journey.narrative_description: Candace and her family notice mood changes, insomnia, and increasingly disorganized thinking. Initial consultations with a campus mental health counselor result in a provisional anxiety and stress diagnosis. Candace complies but feels that something more serious is being missed.
journey.quote: I knew it wasn't just stress. I've been stressed before. This was different.

events.label: Dismissed as Anxiety
events.description: Campus counselor attributes symptoms to graduate school stress. Candace is given coping strategies but no medical referral, delaying workup by several weeks.
events.tooltip: Stress and anxiety misdiagnosis delayed medical workup by several weeks, allowing symptoms to worsen unchecked.

sentiment_reasons: Symptoms are attributed to stress, creating false normalcy
Candace's own intuition conflicts with the clinical assessment
Family is beginning to notice changes but no formal concern is raised

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Mounting unease about dismissed symptoms produces low-grade but persistent distress.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Initial trust in the campus counselor is intact but Candace privately questions the framing.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Candace is self-aware enough to sense misalignment but lacks the clinical vocabulary to challenge it.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Academic and care logistics are still manageable — this has not yet become a crisis.

journey.narrative_description: Within weeks, Candace experiences auditory hallucinations, catatonic episodes, and a witnessed seizure in her apartment. Her partner calls 911. She is admitted to an inpatient psychiatric unit before transfer to a general neurology service. Candace has limited memory of this period.
journey.quote: I barely remember the first few weeks. I've had to piece it together from my mom's notes.

events.label: 911 Call / Emergency Admission
events.description: Partner calls 911 after witnessed tonic-clonic seizure. Candace is transported by ambulance and admitted to an inpatient psychiatric unit before neurology transfer.
events.tooltip: Witnessed seizure triggers 911 call. Admitted to psychiatric unit before neurology transfer — a common and costly misrouting.

events.label: Routed to Psychiatric Unit First
events.description: Initial psychiatric placement delays neurological workup by 48–72 hours. Symptom escalation during this window increases severity of the acute episode.
events.tooltip: Psychiatric placement delays neurological workup 48–72 hours, worsening the acute episode before a correct unit transfer.

sentiment_reasons: Rapid escalation to psychosis and seizure is traumatic and terrifying
Candace loses cognitive continuity during this period
Family is thrust into an emergency without preparation or understanding

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: An acute psychiatric emergency represents the lowest emotional point — terror, confusion, and loss of self.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Psychiatric misrouting before neurology transfer introduces early distrust in the system's ability to identify her condition.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Candace is cognitively absent during this stage — she cannot participate in her own care.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Emergency admission collapses all normal logistics — academic, social, and personal capacity drops to zero.

journey.narrative_description: Candace is intubated and receives high-dose steroids and IVIG in the ICU. Her mother becomes the primary decision-maker during this period. The medical team explains anti-NMDAR encephalitis to her family, though Candace herself is not cognitively present for these conversations.
journey.quote: My mom said the doctors finally had a name for it. I was there but I wasn't really there.

events.label: ICU Intubation
events.description: Respiratory compromise leads to intubation. Candace is placed on mechanical ventilation and receives high-dose steroids and IVIG. Her mother becomes sole medical decision-maker.
events.tooltip: Respiratory compromise leads to intubation in the ICU. Candace's mother becomes sole medical decision-maker.

events.label: Anti-NMDAR Antibody Confirmed
events.description: CSF analysis confirms anti-NMDA receptor antibodies. The team delivers a definitive diagnosis to Candace's family, replacing weeks of diagnostic uncertainty with a named condition and a treatment roadmap.
events.tooltip: CSF analysis confirms anti-NMDA receptor antibodies — replacing weeks of diagnostic uncertainty with a named condition and treatment roadmap.

sentiment_reasons: ICU admission and intubation represent extreme medical severity
Candace is not cognitively present for the diagnostic disclosure
Her mother becomes the sole decision-maker, adding caregiver stress to the system

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Candace is sedated and absent, but retrospective awareness of this period generates deep grief and anxiety.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The ICU team's competence and the eventual confirmed diagnosis begin to rebuild some institutional trust.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Candace cannot advocate for herself — her agency is entirely delegated to her mother.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: All capacity is consumed by acute medical management; normal life is fully suspended.

journey.narrative_description: Imaging and surgical evaluation reveal no underlying teratoma, which the team presents as a favorable prognostic signal. Candace's family receives this news with cautious relief. Candace begins a slow return to awareness and can hold brief conversations.
journey.quote: No tumor. That was the first thing that felt like good news.

events.label: Teratoma Ruled Out
events.description: Pelvic MRI and surgical oncology consultation confirm no underlying ovarian teratoma. The medical team presents this as a favorable prognostic signal. Candace's family exhales.
events.tooltip: No underlying teratoma found on imaging. Team presents this as a favorable prognostic signal; family receives first genuinely good news.

events.label: Candace Regains Awareness
events.description: Candace begins holding brief, coherent conversations for the first time in weeks. She recognizes her mother and partner. Care team marks this as a meaningful clinical milestone.
events.tooltip: Candace holds brief, coherent conversations for the first time in weeks — a key clinical milestone marking neurological stabilization.

sentiment_reasons: Absence of teratoma is the first unambiguously good news
Candace begins to regain awareness and can recognize family members
Cautious optimism emerges but the long road ahead is still unclear

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: The first positive clinical finding — no tumor — generates meaningful emotional relief.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The oncology and neurology team's thoroughness increases confidence in the diagnostic process.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Candace begins to return but cannot yet process complex information or participate in decisions.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Still inpatient — logistical capacity remains severely constrained despite improving medical status.

journey.narrative_description: After incomplete response to first-line therapy, the neurology team initiates rituximab. Candace is now cognitively present enough to be partially involved in consent discussions, though she relies heavily on her mother. She experiences fatigue and nausea but gradually stabilizes.
journey.quote: They explained what rituximab was. I remember nodding and not really absorbing all of it.

events.label: Incomplete Response to First-Line Therapy
events.description: Steroids and IVIG do not produce sufficient clinical improvement. The team escalates to rituximab, extending hospitalization and introducing new side effect concerns for Candace and her family.
events.tooltip: First-line steroids and IVIG produce insufficient improvement, requiring escalation to rituximab and extending hospitalization.

events.label: Stabilization on Rituximab
events.description: After two rituximab infusions, Candace shows measurable neurological improvement. Cognitive responsiveness increases and motor symptoms begin to recede. The care team considers discharge planning.
events.tooltip: Two rituximab infusions yield measurable neurological improvement. Cognitive responsiveness increases and discharge planning begins.

sentiment_reasons: First-line failure introduces new uncertainty and extends hospitalization
Rituximab stabilization eventually provides measurable reassurance
Candace can partially participate in consent but relies heavily on her mother

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Incomplete first-line response triggers anxiety, but subsequent stabilization prevents a deeper emotional slide.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The team's willingness to escalate treatment demonstrates clinical attentiveness, sustaining trust.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Partial cognitive return allows Candace to begin absorbing information, though processing is still impaired.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Extended hospitalization continues to disrupt all external obligations and family resources.

journey.narrative_description: After nearly four months, Candace is discharged with an outpatient immunology follow-up plan and neuropsychological testing scheduled. Her family receives limited structured guidance on what recovery should look like at home. She moves back with her mother.
journey.quote: They said I was stable enough to go home. I didn't feel stable — I just wasn't ICU-level anymore.

events.label: Discharge Without Recovery Roadmap
events.description: Candace is discharged stable but without structured written guidance on what recovery milestones to expect, what symptoms warrant re-admission, or how to access mental health support for post-encephalitis syndrome.
events.tooltip: Discharged without written recovery milestones, re-admission criteria, or mental health referral — leaving Candace and her family without a structured roadmap.

sentiment_reasons: Discharge without a structured recovery roadmap creates anxiety about what comes next
Candace does not feel medically stable, only less acute
Family receives minimal guidance on expected recovery milestones

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: The gap between 'stable enough to discharge' and 'feeling recovered' produces lingering emotional uncertainty.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Discharge without clear written guidance slightly reduces trust in care continuity planning.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Candace is cognitively present but unsure how to manage symptoms or recognize warning signs at home.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Transitioning home introduces new logistical demands — follow-up scheduling, medication management, and family coordination.

journey.narrative_description: Neuropsychological testing reveals deficits in processing speed, working memory, and verbal fluency. Candace finds the results both validating and devastating — the data confirms what she has been experiencing, but also documents how far she is from her prior baseline. She begins occupational and speech therapy.
journey.quote: Seeing the numbers made it real. I knew I was slower, but now I had proof.

events.label: Neuropsych Testing Reveals Deficits
events.description: Formal testing documents significant impairments in processing speed, working memory, and verbal fluency relative to pre-illness baseline. Candace finds the results validating but devastating.
events.tooltip: Testing documents significant deficits in processing speed, working memory, and verbal fluency — confirming what Candace felt but making it clinically undeniable.

events.label: Begins Occupational + Speech Therapy
events.description: Candace begins a structured rehabilitation program. Early sessions are cognitively exhausting but begin to show incremental improvement in word retrieval and task sequencing.
events.tooltip: Structured rehab program begins. Early sessions are exhausting but show incremental gains in word retrieval and task sequencing.

sentiment_reasons: Neuropsychological testing confirms deficits that Candace already suspected
Documentation of how far she is from baseline is both validating and devastating
Beginning structured rehabilitation introduces purpose and a recovery framework

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Having deficits clinically confirmed is emotionally mixed — validation plus confrontation with loss.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The rehabilitation team's structured approach to her recovery increases trust in the outpatient care system.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Formal assessment results give Candace a factual baseline she can use to track progress.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Outpatient therapy is manageable but requires ongoing scheduling coordination across multiple providers.

journey.narrative_description: Every new headache, word-retrieval failure, or sleep disturbance triggers fear of relapse. Candace begins tracking symptoms obsessively in a notes app and contacts her neurologist frequently. The care team provides reassurance but limited structured guidance on relapse warning signs.

inflection_detail.label: Hypervigilance Inflection
inflection_detail.description: Candace's post-discharge symptom monitoring behavior reflects both high health literacy and unmanaged relapse anxiety. This is a critical window where structured support could channel her vigilance productively — or, without intervention, escalate to avoidance and psychological burden.
inflection_detail.sponsor_opportunity: Provide a structured relapse early-warning framework — co-developed with patients — that distinguishes normal recovery fluctuation from actionable warning signs. A digital symptom tracker with embedded clinical guidance could reduce anxiety-driven contacts while increasing meaningful signal capture.
inflection_detail.data_needed: Percentage of anti-NMDAR encephalitis patients who experience clinically documented relapse within 24 months; rate of anxiety disorder comorbidity in post-encephalitis populations.

paths.label: Structured Monitoring Engagement
paths.outcome: Candace's vigilance is formalized into a symptom-tracking protocol with her care team. She receives psychoeducation on distinguishing recovery variation from relapse signals, reducing acute anxiety while maintaining appropriate monitoring. She becomes an engaged candidate for prevention-focused clinical trials.
paths.key_driver: Clear relapse criteria provided by HCP team; access to a nurse navigator or disease-specific patient coordinator; peer connection with other anti-NMDAR encephalitis survivors.

paths.label: Anxiety Escalation and Disengagement
paths.outcome: Without structured guidance, Candace's self-monitoring becomes destabilizing. She begins avoiding activities that could trigger perceived symptoms, withdraws from academic reintegration, and grows skeptical of trial participation due to fear of adverse events destabilizing her fragile recovery.
paths.key_driver: Lack of post-discharge relapse education; no mental health co-management; perceived dismissiveness from neurology team regarding ongoing anxiety.

journey.quote: Every time something feels off, I spiral. Is this the beginning of another episode? How would I even know?

events.label: Relapse Fear Triggers Symptom Spiral
events.description: A minor headache and word-retrieval lapse send Candace into hours of obsessive symptom tracking and an urgent call to her neurologist. No relapse is occurring, but the anxiety becomes debilitating.
events.tooltip: A minor headache triggers hours of obsessive symptom tracking and an urgent call to her neurologist — no relapse, but the anxiety becomes destabilizing.

events.label: No Structured Relapse Warning Protocol
events.description: Candace's care team provides verbal reassurance but no written framework distinguishing normal recovery fluctuation from true relapse signals. This gap sustains her anxiety and generates repeated unplanned contacts.
events.tooltip: No written framework provided to distinguish normal recovery fluctuation from true relapse signals, sustaining hypervigilance and unplanned care contacts.

sentiment_reasons: Every normal symptom triggers fear that it is a relapse signal
Obsessive monitoring becomes exhausting and destabilizing
The care team provides verbal reassurance but no structured framework

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Hypervigilance and relapse anxiety produce persistent negative emotional baseline despite physical stability.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Repeated unscheduled contacts with the neurology team sustain trust but reveal gaps in proactive relapse guidance.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Candace is highly engaged in monitoring but lacks a clinical framework that would make her vigilance productive.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Anxiety-driven contacts and self-monitoring are consuming significant mental and logistical bandwidth.

journey.narrative_description: Candace discovers the Autoimmune Encephalitis Alliance and multiple patient-run online communities. Reading others' recovery narratives provides her first real sense of what the trajectory can look like. She begins contributing, sharing her own experience, and asking detailed questions about treatment maintenance and trial options.
journey.quote: I found people who had been where I was and made it back further than I thought was possible. That changed everything.

events.label: Finds Autoimmune Encephalitis Alliance
events.description: Candace discovers the AE Alliance and connects with other anti-NMDAR encephalitis survivors online. Reading full recovery stories for the first time fundamentally shifts her sense of what is possible.
events.tooltip: Connecting with AE Alliance survivors — including people who returned to graduate school — fundamentally shifts Candace's sense of what recovery is possible.

events.label: Peer Mentorship with Fellow Survivor
events.description: An AE Alliance volunteer who recovered from anti-NMDAR encephalitis and returned to graduate school reaches out directly. Their exchange becomes a monthly touchpoint that sustains Candace's motivation during cognitively difficult weeks.
events.tooltip: Monthly peer mentorship with an AE Alliance volunteer who completed a PhD post-encephalitis sustains motivation through cognitively difficult weeks.

sentiment_reasons: Finding others who have recovered from anti-NMDAR encephalitis fundamentally shifts Candace's sense of what is possible
Peer mentorship with a survivor who returned to graduate school provides direct role modeling
Community belonging reduces isolation and sustains motivation

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Community discovery produces the most significant positive emotional shift in Candace's entire journey.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Community members validate the care Candace has received and provide context for the recovery process.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Absorbing peer knowledge about managing residual symptoms substantially boosts Candace's self-management confidence.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Community members share practical strategies for managing appointments, travel, and academic reintegration.

journey.narrative_description: Motivated by community members' experiences and her own desire to access novel maintenance therapies, Candace begins reviewing ClinicalTrials.gov listings. She identifies several trials investigating B-cell depletion maintenance and neonatal Fc receptor (FcRn) antagonists for autoimmune encephalitis but struggles to parse eligibility criteria and endpoints.
journey.quote: I can find the trials. I just can't always tell if I qualify, or what I'd actually be signing up for.

events.label: Identifies Relevant Trial on ClinicalTrials.gov
events.description: Candace finds an active Phase II trial investigating FcRn antagonist maintenance therapy in anti-NMDAR encephalitis. She bookmarks the listing and prepares questions for her next neurology appointment.
events.tooltip: Candace identifies an active Phase II FcRn antagonist maintenance trial on ClinicalTrials.gov and prepares questions for her next neurology appointment.

events.label: Eligibility Criteria Too Complex to Parse
events.description: The inclusion/exclusion criteria reference biomarker thresholds and prior treatment windows Candace cannot evaluate without clinician interpretation. She cannot confirm her own eligibility unaided.
events.tooltip: Inclusion/exclusion criteria reference biomarker thresholds Candace cannot evaluate without clinician help — she cannot confirm eligibility unaided.

sentiment_reasons: Identifying a relevant trial on ClinicalTrials.gov generates genuine excitement
Eligibility criteria are too complex to parse without clinical guidance
